findings.summary: Several BaseChannel and Tool subclass docstrings restate the class name without adding insight.
evidence: DiscordChannel docstring: 'Discord channel using Gateway websocket.' — restates the class name and import path
SlackChannel docstring: 'Slack channel using Socket Mode.' — same pattern
ReadFileTool docstring: 'Tool to read file contents.' — the name 'ReadFileTool' already conveys this
ExecTool docstring: 'Tool to execute shell commands.' — identical pattern across all 12 Tool subclasses
findings.suggestion: Remove one-line docstrings that restate the class name. Reserve docstrings for non-obvious behavior, caveats, or usage notes (e.g. WhatsAppChannel's bridge architecture note is genuinely useful).

findings.summary: BaseChannel methods have multi-line Args docstrings on parameters whose types and names are already self-documenting.
evidence: is_allowed() has a 5-line docstring for a 1-param method 'sender_id: The sender's identifier.' — the type hint str already says this
_handle_message() has a 10-line Args section documenting 'content: Message text content', 'media: Optional list of media URLs' which are self-evident from type hints
send() has 4-line docstring for single-param method: 'msg: The message to send' — adds nothing beyond the type hint
findings.suggestion: Trim docstrings on base class methods to a single purpose line. The Args sections restate type hints; remove them and keep only non-obvious constraints like 'session_key: Optional session key override (e.g. thread-scoped sessions)' which adds real context.

findings.summary: config/loader.py uses bare print() for error output instead of the project's loguru logger.
evidence: Line 33: print(f'Warning: Failed to load config from {path}: {e}')
Line 34: print('Using default configuration.')
Every other nanobot-src module uses 'from loguru import logger' for structured logging
findings.suggestion: Replace the two print() calls with logger.warning() to maintain consistent logging with the rest of the nanobot-src package.

findings.summary: nanobot/utils/helpers.py has multi-line Args/Returns docstrings on trivial 2-5 line utility functions.
evidence: get_workspace_path() has a 7-line docstring (Args + Returns) for a 4-line function body
parse_session_key() has a 6-line docstring (Args + Returns) for a 3-line function body
timestamp() docstring 'Get current timestamp in ISO format.' is reasonable, but the others are disproportionate
findings.suggestion: Reduce multi-line docstrings to single-line summaries for trivial utility functions. The type hints already document args and returns.

findings.summary: The _split_message() function is independently implemented in telegram.py and discord.py with nearly identical logic.
evidence: telegram.py:81 defines _split_message(content, max_len=4000) with line-break-preferring chunking
discord.py:23 defines _split_message(content, max_len=MAX_MESSAGE_LEN) with identical chunking logic
Both functions: find last newline, fall back to space, fall back to hard cut — same algorithm
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared split_message(content, max_len) function into channels/base.py or a channels/utils.py module, parameterized by the platform's max message length.

findings.summary: AgentLoop.__init__ accepts 14 parameters, making it a wide parameter bag despite the from_config classmethod mitigating some pain.
evidence: AgentLoop.__init__ has 14 named params: bus, provider, workspace, model, max_iterations, temperature, max_tokens, memory_window, brave_api_key, exec_config, cron_service, restrict_to_workspace, session_manager, mcp_servers, channels_config
The from_config classmethod centralizes unpacking but doesn't reduce the init surface — the wide signature persists for direct callers
Parameters like brave_api_key, temperature, max_tokens, memory_window could be grouped into a config/defaults object
findings.suggestion: Consider accepting an AgentDefaults or similar config object for the LLM-related knobs (model, temperature, max_tokens, memory_window, max_iterations) to reduce the init signature to ~8 params. The from_config pattern already exists and could construct this intermediate object.

findings.summary: All 12 Tool subclasses repeat the same @property boilerplate for name, description, and parameters instead of using class attributes.
evidence: base.py defines name, description, parameters as @property @abstractmethod — requiring 3 boilerplate property methods per subclass
WebSearchTool and WebFetchTool already use class attributes directly (name = 'web_search', description = '...', parameters = {...}) without @property, proving the pattern works
ReadFileTool, WriteFileTool, EditFileTool, ListDirTool, ExecTool, MessageTool, SpawnTool, CronTool all implement 3 @property methods each, totaling ~108 lines of pure boilerplate
findings.suggestion: Change the base Tool class to accept name/description/parameters as regular class attributes (not abstract properties). WebSearchTool and WebFetchTool already demonstrate this works. This eliminates ~9 lines per tool class.

findings.summary: CronService manually serializes/deserializes CronJob dataclasses to/from JSON dicts instead of using Pydantic or dataclass-based serialization.
evidence: _load_store() manually constructs CronJob, CronSchedule, CronPayload, CronJobState from nested dict access over 30 lines (lines 86-113)
_save_store() manually serializes back to nested dicts over 30 lines (lines 130-165)
The config module already uses Pydantic models with model_validate/model_dump for the same serialization pattern
findings.suggestion: Convert CronJob and related types to Pydantic models (the project already depends on Pydantic) and use model_validate/model_dump for serialization. This would replace ~60 lines of manual dict construction with ~2 lines each.

findings.summary: Backend uses stdlib logging while nanobot-src uses loguru — two logging frameworks in the same project with no interop bridge.
evidence: All 6 backend/app modules use 'import logging; logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)'
All 20+ nanobot-src modules use 'from loguru import logger'
config/loader.py uses neither — it uses bare print() (separate finding)
No loguru intercept handler or stdlib-to-loguru bridge configured in the codebase
findings.suggestion: Choose one logging strategy for the project. Since nanobot-src is the larger package and already standardized on loguru, the simplest path is adding a loguru intercept handler in the backend's startup to unify output. Alternatively, if the backend/app is intentionally a separate service, document the convention boundary.

findings.summary: Tool subclasses use two incompatible patterns for defining name/description/parameters: abstract @property methods vs class-level attributes.
evidence: WebSearchTool and WebFetchTool define name, description, parameters as class-level attributes: 'name = "web_search"', 'parameters = {...}'
ReadFileTool, WriteFileTool, EditFileTool, ListDirTool, ExecTool, MessageTool, SpawnTool, CronTool all define them as @property methods
Both patterns work because Python properties and class attributes are both accessible via self.name, but the inconsistency is surprising
findings.suggestion: Standardize all tool subclasses to use one pattern. Class-level attributes are simpler (less boilerplate) — migrate the @property-based tools to match WebSearchTool/WebFetchTool. Update Tool base class to not require abstract properties.

findings.summary: The channels_status CLI command hardcodes 9 channel display blocks instead of iterating over channel config like ChannelManager._CHANNEL_DEFS does.
evidence: cli/commands.py lines 611-688: channels_status() has 9 sequential blocks — one per channel — each with custom display logic
channels/manager.py lines 37-50: _CHANNEL_DEFS is a data-driven list that ChannelManager iterates over to initialize channels
Adding a new channel requires updating both the data-driven _CHANNEL_DEFS and the hardcoded status table — they will drift
findings.suggestion: Make channels_status() data-driven by iterating over the channel configs (or a shared channel metadata list) rather than having one block per channel. Each channel could expose a display_config() method or the metadata could include display hints.

findings.summary: Package __init__.py files have inconsistent content: some have docstrings, one is empty, and none export symbols.
evidence: channels/__init__.py: '"""Chat channels module with plugin architecture."""'
agent/__init__.py: '"""Agent core module."""'
providers/__init__.py: '"""LLM provider abstraction module."""'
backend/app/routes/__init__.py: empty file (no docstring)
None of these __init__.py files export any symbols — they all serve only as package markers
findings.suggestion: Pick one convention and apply it consistently: either all __init__.py files have a module docstring, or none do. Since these are all pure marker files, the minimal approach (empty or one-line docstring) would be cleanest. Add a docstring to routes/__init__.py or remove docstrings from the others.
